content.text: Team Health Scorecard Metrics for GenAI Teams

Traditional software engineering scorecards measure deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, and MTTR — the DORA metrics. GenAI teams need all of these plus a second layer that tracks model-specific health: eval pass rates, token cost efficiency, hallucination rates, and safety compliance. Without this second layer, a team can score green on all DORA metrics while silently shipping degraded model quality to production.

A GenAI team health scorecard aggregates metrics across six dimensions into a weekly or monthly view that engineering leads, platform teams, and product stakeholders can interpret without needing to understand every underlying system. The scorecard does not replace dashboards — it summarises them.

attrs.alt: GenAI Team Health Scorecard — Six Dimensions

content.text: Dimension 1 — Quality and Reliability

Quality metrics measure whether the service is producing correct, safe, and consistent outputs. They are the GenAI-specific complement to traditional reliability metrics like error rate and availability.
Metric
Definition
Target
Collection Method
Eval pass rate
Percentage of eval suite cases passing in CI across the trailing 30 days
CI eval runner; aggregated by scripts/eval-summary.py
Hallucination rate
Percentage of sampled production responses flagged as factually incorrect by an LLM judge
Online eval sample: 200 requests/day through a judge prompt
Safety baseline pass rate
Percentage of adversarial safety evals passing
Weekly adversarial eval run; blocks release if <100%
Structured output parse error rate
Percentage of tool-call or structured-output responses that fail to parse per the declared schema
Application metrics; emitted as a counter on each parse attempt
Quality regression incidents
Count of production incidents caused by model output quality degradation
0 per quarter
Incident post-mortems; tagged 'quality-regression'

The hallucination rate metric is the hardest to collect reliably. An online judge that samples 200 production requests per day provides a directional signal but not a precise measurement. Calibrate the judge prompt against a labelled dataset of known-good and known-hallucinated responses to establish its precision and recall before using it as a scorecard input.

Eval pass rate measures the eval suite's assessment of quality, not actual user-perceived quality. A high eval pass rate with a poor hallucination rate means the eval suite is not covering the cases that matter. The two metrics should be tracked together as a cross-check.
Dimension 2 — Cost Efficiency

Cost metrics measure whether the team is using token budget wisely. LLM inference costs are directly proportional to token usage, and uncontrolled growth in token consumption frequently tracks with degrading prompt discipline rather than increasing feature complexity.
Metric
Definition
Target
Collection Method
Cost per successful request (CPR)
Total inference spend divided by successfully completed requests (excluding error responses)
Defined per feature; tracked for ≤10% month-over-month growth
Billing API; request counter from application metrics
Token utilisation ratio
Input tokens actually used for reasoning vs. total input tokens (input + padding + template boilerplate)
Computed from span attributes: prompt_tokens / (prompt_tokens + template_overhead)
Prompt cache hit rate
Percentage of input tokens served from prompt cache
>40% for services with stable system prompts
Provider API response headers or usage metadata
Monthly spend vs budget
Actual LLM spend as percentage of the team's allocated budget
<90% (alert at 80%)
Cloud billing export; Grafana alert
Cost per eval pass
Total eval-run cost divided by number of passing eval cases
Tracked; alert if trending upward
Eval runner cost logging; CI cost report

Cost per successful request is the most actionable cost metric because it normalises spend to value delivered. A service whose CPR is rising while request volume is flat has a prompt efficiency problem — inputs are getting longer or model calls are being chained unnecessarily. A service whose CPR is falling while quality metrics hold steady has improved its prompt efficiency.
Dimension 3 — Security and Compliance
Security metrics measure adherence to the dependency policy, secrets hygiene, and vulnerability posture. For GenAI systems, they extend to prompt-injection exposure and data-handling compliance.
Metric
Definition
Target
Collection Method
Mean age of open HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs
Average days since disclosure for unpatched HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs in the service's dependency graph
0 days (no open HIGH/CRITICAL)
pip-audit / Grype in CI; Dependency-Track dashboard
Secrets leak incidents
Count of secrets (API keys, tokens) detected in commits or logs
git-secrets pre-commit hook; CI secret scanner
Approved-dependency coverage
Percentage of production dependencies present in the allowlist
CI allowlist gate; reported in dependency-policy job
Active waiver count
Number of currently active vulnerability waivers
<3 (growing count signals deferred patching)
scripts/waiver_report.py; weekly CI job
Prompt injection test pass rate
Percentage of adversarial prompt-injection test cases that the service correctly refuses or ignores
Monthly red-team eval run
Dimension 4 — Delivery Velocity
Delivery velocity metrics are the standard DORA four, contextualised for GenAI services where deployments include not just code but prompt versions and model configurations.
Metric
Definition
Target (Elite)
Notes
Deploy frequency
Number of production deployments per week
≥1 per day
Count deployments that include any change (code, prompt, model config)
Lead time: commit → production
Median time from commit merge to production deployment
<1 hour
Measure from merge timestamp to deployment timestamp in CI
Change failure rate
Percentage of deployments that cause a degradation requiring rollback or hotfix
Rollbacks + hotfix PRs / total deployments
MTTR
Median time from incident detection to service restoration
<1 hour
Incident tracker; from 'incident opened' to 'resolved'

For GenAI teams, the change failure rate metric should tag the failure cause: code regression, prompt regression, model regression, or dependency regression. Prompt regressions are particularly common and are easily missed if deployments are only tracked at the code level. A deployment that changes only the system prompt file counts as a deployment and its failure counts toward the change failure rate.
Dimension 5 — Observability

Observability metrics measure whether the team can see what is happening in production and respond quickly. An unobservable GenAI service cannot be debugged, which means quality regressions go undetected until users report them.
Metric
Definition
Target
Distributed trace coverage
Percentage of production requests that produce a complete trace (entry → model call → response)
Alert signal-to-noise ratio
Ratio of actionable alerts to total alerts fired in the trailing 30 days
>80% actionable
Runbook coverage
Percentage of active alerts that have a linked runbook with a defined response procedure
Eval feature coverage
Percentage of production features (prompt chains, tool types) covered by at least one eval case
Dashboard staleness
Days since any production dashboard panel was last reviewed and confirmed accurate
<30 days

Alert signal-to-noise ratio below 80% is a serious health signal. If on-call engineers are seeing mostly noise, they start ignoring or silencing alerts — including the ones that matter. When the ratio drops below 80%, schedule a dedicated alert review session to identify and remove low-value alerts before the next on-call rotation.
Dimension 6 — Process Maturity

Process maturity metrics measure whether the team is following the engineering standards that prevent accumulated technical debt. For GenAI teams, this includes eval coverage in the definition of done, RFC discipline for dependencies and architecture changes, and post-incident review follow-through.
Metric
Definition
Target
Eval-in-DoD coverage rate
Percentage of features shipped in the last quarter that included at least one new eval case
RFC filing rate
Percentage of dependency additions and architecture changes that were preceded by an approved RFC
Post-incident review completion
Percentage of P1/P2 incidents that received a written PIR within 5 business days
On-call rotation health
Number of engineers in the primary rotation (min for healthy coverage)
Tech debt ratio
Percentage of sprint capacity spent on tech debt vs feature work
GenAI-Specific Metric Definitions and Rationale

The six dimensions above each contain metrics that need precise operational definitions to be useful. Ambiguous metrics produce disagreement about whether a number is good or bad, which stalls action. The following sections define the most commonly misunderstood GenAI-specific metrics in detail.
Eval Pass Rate — Operational Definition

Eval pass rate is the percentage of deterministic eval cases that produce a passing result when run against the current service build. It is computed per CI run and aggregated over a 30-day rolling window for the scorecard.
Three common mistakes in computing eval pass rate:
Including non-deterministic evals in the aggregate.

LLM-judged evals have natural variance. A case that passes 80% of the time is not a 100%-pass or a 0%-pass case — it is a noisy signal. Track deterministic evals (schema validation, exact-match, regex-match) separately from fuzzy evals (LLM judge scores). The scorecard should report the deterministic pass rate; the fuzzy pass rate is an overlay metric.
Counting skipped cases as passes.

When CI skips eval cases due to flakiness or time budget constraints, skipped cases must not inflate the pass rate. The denominator is the number of cases attempted, not the number of cases in the suite. Log skipped cases separately.
Not versioning the eval suite.

The eval suite evolves as the service evolves. A pass rate computed against v1 of the suite is not comparable to one computed against v3. Tag eval runs with the suite version and compare only within the same version when reporting trends.
Cost Per Successful Request — Calculation
Cost per successful request (CPR) is the primary cost efficiency metric. It is calculated as:
CPR = total_inference_spend / successful_request_count
Where:
total_inference_spend
is the LLM API cost attributable to the service in the measurement period (typically one week), derived from the provider's billing API or cost-allocation tags.
successful_request_count
is the number of requests that returned a valid, parsed response to the caller — excluding 4xx errors, timeout responses, and requests that triggered the fallback path.

The reason for dividing by successful requests (not total requests) is that failed requests represent pure waste — they consumed tokens without delivering value. A service with a high error rate looks artificially cheap if errors are counted in the denominator.
Track CPR alongside request volume. A rising CPR with flat volume signals prompt bloat. A flat CPR with rising volume is healthy — the marginal cost per request is stable as the service scales.

# scripts/compute_cpr.py — run weekly from CI
import json
from datetime import date, timedelta
from pathlib import Path

def compute_cpr(billing_export_path: str, metrics_export_path: str) -> dict:
    """
    billing_export: list of {date, service, cost_usd}
    metrics_export: list of {date, service, successful_requests, total_requests}
    """
    billing = json.loads(Path(billing_export_path).read_text())
    metrics = json.loads(Path(metrics_export_path).read_text())

    seven_days_ago = date.today() - timedelta(days=7)
    service_name = "genai-service"

    total_spend = sum(
        b["cost_usd"] for b in billing
        if b["service"] == service_name
        and date.fromisoformat(b["date"]) >= seven_days_ago
    )
    total_success = sum(
        m["successful_requests"] for m in metrics
        if m["service"] == service_name
        and date.fromisoformat(m["date"]) >= seven_days_ago
    )

    cpr = total_spend / total_success if total_success > 0 else float("inf")
    return {
        "period_spend_usd": round(total_spend, 4),
        "successful_requests": total_success,
        "cpr_usd": round(cpr, 6),
    }
Trace Coverage — Why 100% Is Non-Negotiable

Trace coverage measures the percentage of production requests that produce a complete distributed trace — from the HTTP entry point through all model API calls to the final response. A target of 100% seems ambitious but is achievable and necessary.
Without full trace coverage, two failure modes become invisible:
Latency spikes on the model API path.
If 10% of requests are not traced, a model provider having a bad day (slow responses, rate limiting) may not appear in the p99 latency chart — the affected requests are the untraced ones.
Prompt injection via tool call results.
If a tool call is not traced, there is no record of what input it received and what output it produced. Prompt injection attacks that exploit tool results are invisible without this trace data.
The instrumentation required for 100% trace coverage is straightforward: wrap the LLM client call in an OpenTelemetry span and propagate the trace context through async boundaries. The
opentelemetry-instrumentation-openai
package does this automatically for the OpenAI SDK; equivalent packages exist for Anthropic and Bedrock.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.openai import OpenAIInstrumentor

# Auto-instrument — all openai calls produce spans automatically
OpenAIInstrumentor().instrument()

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

async def generate_response(user_input: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("generate_response") as span:
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.model", "gpt-4o")
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.request.input_length", len(user_input))

        response = await client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_input}],
        )

        output = response.choices[0].message.content
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.response.output_length", len(output))
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.input_tokens", response.usage.prompt_tokens)
        span.set_attribute("gen_ai.usage.output_tokens", response.usage.completion_tokens)
        return output
Collecting and Computing the Scorecard

A scorecard is only useful if it is computed automatically and published on a predictable cadence. Manual scorecard compilation takes hours and introduces selection bias — teams tend to emphasise metrics that are doing well. The recommended implementation:
Data Collection Script

Extend the script with additional metric collectors for each dimension. Publish the resulting JSON to an S3 bucket or internal metrics store; feed it into Grafana or a Notion database for stakeholder visibility.
Scoring and Thresholds
Raw metric values are useful for debugging but not for at-a-glance health assessment. The scorecard assigns each metric a RAG (Red/Amber/Green) status based on thresholds:
Status
Meaning
Action
Green
Metric is within target range
No action required; track for trend
Amber
Metric is outside target but within warning threshold
Team lead investigates; improvement plan within one sprint
Red
Metric is outside warning threshold
Escalate to engineering manager; block new feature work if sustained for >2 weeks
Example threshold configuration for the eval pass rate metric:

{
  "metric": "eval_pass_rate",
  "unit": "percent",
  "green": {"min": 95},
  "amber": {"min": 90, "max": 95},
  "red": {"max": 90},
  "owner": "platform-team",
  "review_cadence": "weekly"
}
Store threshold configurations in a
scorecard-config.json
file in the repository. This makes the thresholds visible, reviewable, and adjustable as the team's baseline improves.
Scorecard Anti-Patterns to Avoid
Several failure modes appear repeatedly in teams that implement scorecards. Each one reduces the scorecard's value without appearing to be a problem on the surface.
Goodhart's Law — When the Metric Becomes the Target

Goodhart's Law states: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." GenAI scorecards are particularly susceptible because many metrics can be gamed without improving the underlying system:
Metric
How It Gets Gamed
Detection
Eval pass rate
Team removes failing evals instead of fixing the underlying issue; easy cases added to inflate the count
Track eval suite size alongside pass rate; review diffs when pass rate suddenly jumps
Hallucination rate
Judge prompt is tuned to pass more responses; production sample is biased toward easy requests
Spot-check judge verdicts against human labels monthly; verify sample is random
Deploy frequency
Small meaningless changes deployed to inflate count (README updates, whitespace)
Measure deploys that include substantive changes; exclude docs-only deploys
Cost per request
Expensive features are moved off the main service to a separate service, reducing CPR for the measured service
Track total team spend alongside per-service CPR; alert on split-service patterns
Alert signal-to-noise ratio
Low-value alerts are silenced or deleted rather than fixed
Track total alert count alongside the ratio; decreasing total count with improving ratio is a red flag

The antidote to Goodhart's Law is to always track related metrics in pairs. An improving pass rate alongside a shrinking eval suite is a red flag. A falling CPR alongside a new microservice boundary is a red flag. Pair each metric with a context metric that reveals whether the improvement is genuine.
Lagging Indicators Without Leading Indicators

All of the scorecard metrics described above are lagging indicators — they measure what already happened. A production hallucination rate of 3% tells you quality has degraded; it does not tell you it is about to degrade.
Complement the lagging scorecard with leading indicators that give earlier warning:
Eval suite coverage growth rate.
Are new features being added to the eval suite before they ship? A slowing coverage growth rate predicts a future hallucination rate increase.
Prompt complexity score.

Average token length of system prompts across the service. A rising complexity score predicts rising CPR and potentially rising hallucination rates if prompts are growing through accumulation rather than deliberate design.
Dependency upgrade lag.
How many approved packages are behind their target version? A growing lag predicts a future CVE exposure event.
On-call ticket volume trend.
Rising ticket volume in the week before a scorecard Red metric appears is a leading signal that the metric is about to cross its threshold.
Missing Context in Red Metrics

A Red metric without context creates alarm without direction. When publishing the scorecard, always include the trailing trend (last four weeks), the most recent event that affected the metric, and the team's current mitigation. A scorecard that shows eval pass rate at 88% (Red) alongside "[3 days ago] new adversarial test cases added; fix in progress, expected Green in 5 days" is useful. A scorecard that just shows 88% in red creates panic.

Automate the context annotations: generate a change log entry each time a metric's RAG status changes, linking to the CI run or incident that caused the change. Store these in the scorecard JSON so the dashboard can display them alongside the current value.
Using the Scorecard in Practice
A scorecard that is generated but not reviewed is just a log file. Build the scorecard into existing team rituals:
Sprint retrospective.
Review the scorecard at the start of every retrospective. Amber and Red metrics become retrospective topics automatically, without relying on anyone to remember to raise them.
Quarterly engineering review.
Trend the scorecard across quarters to show progress on longer-running improvements (eval coverage growth, cost efficiency gains, security debt reduction). Share with engineering leadership.
On-call handoff.
Include the current observability dimension scores in the on-call handoff email. A falling trace coverage score or rising alert noise ratio is actionable before the on-call rotation starts.
New engineer onboarding.
Walk new team members through the scorecard in their first week. It gives them a structured map of the team's engineering priorities without requiring them to read every policy document first.

The scorecard is a conversation starter, not a performance review tool. Amber and Red metrics should trigger curiosity and collaborative problem-solving, not blame assignment. Teams that fear the scorecard hide problems; teams that use it as a shared navigation tool surface and fix problems faster.
Bootstrapping Metrics When Starting From Zero

A team without existing metrics instrumentation faces a bootstrapping challenge: all scorecard metrics appear as "no data" until infrastructure is in place. The recommended sequencing prioritises metrics that are either already available or cheapest to collect:
Week 1 — Delivery velocity.

Git history and CI logs already exist. Deploy frequency, lead time, and change failure rate can be computed from existing data. This gives the team its first scorecard dimension immediately with no new instrumentation.
Week 2 — Eval pass rate.
If an eval suite exists, plumb its results into the CI summary. If not, write three deterministic evals for the most critical user-facing feature. Even three evals give a meaningful baseline.
Week 3 — Security metrics.
Run
pip-audit
in CI and add the allowlist gate. Active CVE count and approved-dependency coverage are now computed on every PR.
Week 4 — Cost metrics.
Add OpenTelemetry span attributes for token counts to the main LLM call. Export to a metrics store (Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch). Cost per request is now computable.
Month 2 — Observability and quality metrics.

Add full distributed tracing (trace coverage), production request sampling for the hallucination judge, and the alert audit (signal-to-noise ratio). These require the most instrumentation work and are lowest priority in the bootstrap sequence.

By the end of month two, all six scorecard dimensions have at least one real metric. The first quarter of scores is the baseline — do not set thresholds until you know what the team's natural performance level is. Set thresholds at the end of the first quarter based on actual observed values, with targets set 10–20% better than the current baseline.
Summary

A GenAI team health scorecard extends the standard DORA metrics with five additional dimensions specific to model-based services: quality and reliability (eval pass rate, hallucination rate), cost efficiency (cost per request, cache hit rate), security and compliance (CVE age, waiver count), observability (trace coverage, alert signal-to-noise), and process maturity (eval-in-DoD coverage, RFC discipline). Metrics are collected automatically via CI scripts and published weekly. RAG thresholds convert raw values to actionable status signals. The scorecard works best when embedded in existing rituals — retrospectives, quarterly reviews, on-call handoffs — rather than reviewed in isolation.
